scheme_candidates.bbox_note: The large, multi-pathway scheme at the top of the page.
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A multi-pathway reaction scheme starting from acetoacetic ester. The first path shows sequential C2 mono- and di-alkylation. The C2 monoalkylated product can be converted to a gamma-diketone or a monosubstituted acetone derivative. The C2 dialkylated product is converted to a disubstituted acetone derivative. A second path from acetoacetic ester shows C4 monoalkylation via a dianion intermediate.
scheme_candidates.caption_text: R1 = 1°, 2° or 3° alkyl, aryl; R2 = 1° or 2° alkyl, allyl, benzyl; R3 = 1° or 2° alkyl, allyl, benzyl; base: NaH, NaOR1, LiHMDS, NaHMDS
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The preparation of ketones via the C-alkylation of esters of 3-oxobutanoic acid (acetoacetic esters) is called the acetoacetic ester synthesis. Acetoacetic esters can be deprotonated at either the C2 or at both the C2 and C4 carbons, depending on the amount of base used. The C-H bonds on the C2 carbon atom are activated by the electron-withdrawing effect of the two neighboring carbonyl groups. These protons are fairly acidic (pKa ~11 for C2 and pKa ~24 for C4), so the C2 position is deprotonated first in the presence of one equivalent of base (sodium alkoxide, LDA, NaHMDS or LiHMDS, etc.). The resulting anion can be trapped with an electrophile (e.g., alkyl halide) is added to the dianion, alkylation occurs first at the most nucleophilic (reactive) C4 position. The resulting alkylated acetoacetic ester derivatives can be subjected to two types of hydrolytic cleavage, depending on the conditions: 1) dilute acid hydrolyzes the ester group, and the resulting β-keto acid undergoes decarboxylation to give a ketone (mono- or disubstituted acetone derivative); 2) aqueous base induces a retro-Claisen reaction to afford acids after protonation. The hydrolysis by dilute acid is most commonly used, since the reaction mixture is not contaminated with by-products derived from ketonic scission. More recently the use of the Krapcho decarboxylation allows neutral decarboxylation conditions. As with malonic ester, monoalkyl derivatives of acetoacetic ester undergo a base-catalyzed coupling reaction in the presence of iodine. Hydrolysis and decarboxylation of the coupled products produce γ-diketones. The starting acetoacetic esters are most often obtained via the Claisen condensation of the corresponding esters, but other methods are also available for their preparation.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: The three-part mechanism scheme at the bottom of the page.
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A three-part reaction mechanism. The first part, 'Alkylation', shows the deprotonation of acetoacetic ester to form an enolate, which then undergoes an SN2 reaction with an alkyl halide to form a C2 alkylated acetoacetic ester. The second part, 'Hydrolysis', shows the acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of the ester to a beta-keto acid. The third part, 'Decarboxylation', shows the loss of CO2 from the beta-keto acid to form an enol, which then tautomerizes to the final ketone product.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Mechanism: The first step is the deprotonation of acetoacetic ester at the C2 position with one equivalent of base. The resulting enolate is nucleophilic and reacts with the electrophilic alkyl halide in an SN2 reaction to afford the C2 substituted acetoacetic ester, which can be isolated. The ester is hydrolyzed by treatment with aqueous acid to the corresponding β-keto acid, which is thermally unstable and undergoes decarboxylation via a six-membered transition state.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A multi-step synthesis of a bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane skeleton, a substructure of Solanoeclepin A, is shown. The synthesis starts with the alkylation of tert-butyl acetoacetate, followed by the formation of a dioxenone precursor. This precursor then undergoes an intramolecular photochemical [2+2] cycloaddition, and the resulting cycloadduct is reduced with LiAlH4 to yield the final diol product.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: In the laboratory of H. Hiemstra, the synthesis of the bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane substructure of solanoeclepin A was undertaken utilizing the intramolecular photochemical dioxenone-alkene [2+2] cycloaddition reaction. The dioxenone precursor was prepared from the commercially available tert-butyl acetoacetate using the acetoacetic ester synthesis. When this dioxenone precursor was subjected to irradiation at 300 nm, complete conversion of the starting material was observed after about 4h, and the expected cycloadduct was formed in acceptable yield.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A two-step synthesis of a 2-hydroxy-3-acetylfuran derivative is depicted. The first step involves the alkylation of t-butylacetoacetate with a bromo-ketone using sodium hydride. The second step is a cyclization reaction promoted by trifluoroacetic acid to form the furan ring.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: R. Neier et al. synthesized substituted 2-hydroxy-3-acetylfurans by the alkylation of tert-butylacetoacetate with an α-haloketone, followed by treatment of the intermediate with trifluoroacetic acid. When furans are prepared from β-ketoesters and α-haloketones, the reaction is known as the Feist-Bénary reaction. A second alkylation of the C2 alkylated intermediate with various bromoalkanes yielded 2,2-disubstituted products, which upon treatment with TFA, provided access to trisubstituted furans.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: Two molecules of an ester with R and R' groups react under conditions of molten sodium metal, TMSCl, and xylene at reflux to form a bis-silyloxyalkene intermediate. This intermediate is then treated with H+/H2O to yield an acyloin product.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The acyloin condensation affords acyloins (α-hydroxy ketones) by treating aliphatic esters with molten, highly dispersed sodium in hot xylene. The resulting disodium acyloin derivatives are acidified to liberate the corresponding acyloins, which are valuable synthetic intermediates. Aliphatic monoesters give symmetrical compounds, while diesters lead to cyclic acyloins. The intramolecular acyloin condensation is one of the best ways of closing rings of 10 members or more (up to 34 membered rings were synthesized). For the preparation of aromatic acyloins (R=Ar), the benzoin condensation between two aromatic aldehydes is applied. The acyloin condensation is performed in an inert atmosphere, since the acyloins and their anions are readily oxidized. For small rings (ring size: 4-6), yields are greatly improved in the presence of TMSCl and by the use of ultrasound. The addition of TMSCl increases the scope of this reaction by preventing base-catalyzed side reactions such as β-elimination, Claisen or Dieckmann condensations. The resulting bis-silyloxyalkenes are either isolated or converted into acyloins by simple hydrolysis or alcoholysis.

extracts.reagents_text: molten sodium metal, TMSCl, H+ / H2O

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: Mechanism A shows the reaction of two ester molecules with sodium, leading to radical anions that dimerize. The resulting dianion eliminates two alkoxide groups to form a diketone. The diketone is further reduced by sodium to a dianion, which upon acidic work-up forms an enediol. The enediol then tautomerizes to the final acyloin product.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Mechanism A: There are currently two proposed mechanisms for the acyloin ester condensation. In mechanism A the sodium reacts with the ester in a single electron transfer (SET) process to give a radical anion species, which can dimerize to a dialkoxy dianion. Elimination of two alkoxide anions gives a diketone. Further reduction (electron transfer from the sodium metal to the diketone) leads to a new dianion, which upon acidic work-up yields an enediol that tautomerizes to an acyloin.

extracts.intermediates_text: radical anion, dialkoxy dianion, diketone, enediol
extracts.notes_text: Mechanism A involves dimerization of radical anions.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: Mechanism B shows the reduction of an ester to a radical anion, which attacks another ester molecule. After elimination and cyclization, an epoxide intermediate is formed. This is further reduced, undergoes ring-opening and elimination to form a diketone. The diketone is then reduced and protonated to an enediol, which tautomerizes to the acyloin product.

scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The aldol reaction involves the addition of the enol/enolate of a carbonyl compound (nucleophile) to an aldehyde or ketone (electrophile). The initial product of the reaction is a β-hydroxycarbonyl compound that under certain conditions undergoes dehydration to generate the corresponding α,β-unsaturated carbonyl compound. The transformation takes its name from 3-hydroxybutanal, the acid-catalyzed self-condensation product of acetaldehyde, which is commonly called aldol.

scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Mechanism: The mechanism of the classical acid catalyzed aldol reaction involves the equilibrium formation of an enol, which functions as a nucleophile. ... Aldol reaction of preformed enolates generally provides the products with high diastereoselectivity, (Z)-enolates yielding the syn product, (E)-enolates forming the anti product as the major diastereomer. The stereochemical outcome of the reaction can be rationalized based on the Zimmerman-Traxler model, according to which the reaction proceeds through a six-membered chairlike transition state.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A chiral auxiliary-mediated aldol reaction is shown. A chiral sultam-derived ketone is enolized with Et2BOTf and i-Pr2NEt, and then reacted with an aldehyde to form a beta-hydroxy ketone product with high diastereoselectivity. This product is an intermediate in the synthesis of (-)-Denticulatin A.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The first enantioselective total synthesis of (–)-denticulatin A was accomplished by W. Oppolzer. The key step in their approach was based on enantiotopic group differentiation in a meso dialdehyde by an aldol reaction. In the aldol reaction they utilized a bornanesultam chiral auxiliary. The enolization of N-propionylbornane-10,2-sultam provided the (Z)-borylenolate derivative, which underwent an aldol reaction with the meso dialdehyde to afford the product with high yield and enantiopurity. In the final stages of the synthesis they utilized a second, double-diastereodifferentiating aldol reaction. Aldol reaction of the (Z)-titanium enolate gave the anti-Felkin syn product. The stereochemical outcome of the reaction was determined by the α-chiral center in the aldehyde component.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: An asymmetric aldol reaction using a chiral borane reagent is depicted. A ketone is treated with a chiral chloroborane and Et3N, followed by reaction with an aldehyde at low temperature to yield a beta-hydroxy ketone product. This is a key step in the synthesis of Rhizoxin D.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: During the total synthesis of rhizoxin D by J.D. White et al., an asymmetric aldol reaction was utilized to achieve the coupling of two key fragments. The aldol reaction of the aldehyde and the chiral enolate derived from (+)-chlorodiisopinocampheylborane afforded the product with a diastereomeric ratio of 17-20:1 at the C13 stereocenter. During their studies, White and co-workers also showed that the stereochemical induction of the chiral boron substituent and the stereocenters present in the enolate reinforce each other thus representing a “matched” aldol reaction.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: The top half of the page illustrates three main types of alkene metathesis: Ring-Closing Metathesis (RCM) and Acyclic Diene Metathesis (ADMET) shown as an equilibrium, Ring-Opening Metathesis Polymerization (ROMP), and Cross-Metathesis (CM).
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The metal-catalyzed redistribution of carbon-carbon double bonds is called alkene (olefin) metathesis. The first report of double-bond scrambling was published in 1955 but the term “olefin metathesis” was introduced only thirteen years later by N. Calderon and co-workers. There are several different olefin metathesis reactions: ring-opening metathesis polymerization (ROMP), ring-closing metathesis (RCM), acyclic diene metathesis polymerization (ADMET), and cross-metathesis (CM or XMET). These various olefin metathesis reactions give access to molecules and polymers that would be difficult to obtain by other means.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: The catalytic cycle for alkene metathesis using a Grubbs-type catalyst is depicted. An initial ruthenium carbene complex reacts with an alkene. The mechanism branches into two pathways (I and II) via ligand exchange, leading to cis and trans metallacyclobutane intermediates, respectively. These intermediates then undergo cycloreversion to release a new alkene product and regenerate a ruthenium carbene species, which re-enters the catalytic cycle.

scheme_candidates.description: The conversion of a carboxylic acid to its homolog (one CH2 group longer) in three stages is called the Arndt-Eistert synthesis. This homologation is the best preparative method for the chain elongation of carboxylic acids. The first stage of the process is the conversion to the corresponding acid chloride. The second stage involves the formation of a α-diazo methylketone, followed by a Wolff rearrangement in the third stage. The third stage is conducted either in the presence of solid silver oxide/water or silver benzoate/triethylamine solution.

Mechanism for the Arndt-Eistert homologation. A carboxylic acid is converted to an acid chloride, which reacts with diazomethane to form an alpha-diazo ketone. The diazo ketone, upon treatment with a silver catalyst, undergoes Wolff rearrangement via a carbene and ketene intermediate. The ketene is trapped by a nucleophile to yield the homologated product.

scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The base catalyzed decomposition of arylsulfonylhydrazones of aldehydes and ketones to provide alkenes is called the Bamford-Stevens reaction. When an organolithium compound is used as the base, the reaction is termed the Shapiro reaction. The most synthetically useful protocol involves treatment of the substrate with at least two equivalents of an organolithium compound (usually MeLi or BuLi) in ether, hexane, or tetramethylethylenediamine. The in situ formed alkenyllithium is then protonated to give the alkene.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A tosylhydrazone anion eliminates ArSO2- to form a diazoalkane. In aprotic solvent, the diazoalkane loses N2 to form a carbene, which rearranges via a [1,2]-H shift to an alkene. In protic solvent, the diazoalkane is protonated, loses N2 to form a carbocation, which then eliminates a proton to form an alkene.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The reaction mechanism depends on the reaction conditions. The reaction of tosylhydrazone with a strong base (usually metal-alkoxides) in protic solvents results in the formation of a diazo compound that in some cases can be isolated. The diazo compound gives rise to a carbocation that may lose a proton or undergo a Wagner-Meerwein rearrangement. Therefore, a complex mixture of products may be isolated. When aprotic conditions are used, the initially formed diazo compound loses a molecule of nitrogen and a carbene intermediate is formed, which either undergoes a [1,2]-H shift or various carbene insertion reactions.

scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Upon treatment with base (e.g., NaOH), α-diketones rearrange to give salts of α-hydroxy acids. This process is called the benzilic acid rearrangement. The reaction takes place with both aliphatic and aromatic α-diketones and α-keto aldehydes. Usually diaryl diketones undergo benzilic acid rearrangements in excellent yields, but aliphatic α-diketones that have enolizable α-protons give low yields due to competing aldol condensation reactions.

Treatment with hydroxide (OH-) initiates a [1,2]-shift and ring contraction to form a cyclic α-hydroxy acid salt intermediate, which is then acidified (H+) to yield the final cyclic α-hydroxy acid with a smaller ring.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Cyclic α-diketones undergo the synthetically useful ring-contraction benzilic acid rearrangement under these conditions. When alkoxides or amide anions are used in place of hydroxides, the corresponding esters and amides are formed. This process is called the benzilic ester rearrangement.

extracts.transformation_text: Ring-contraction of a cyclic α-diketone to a cyclic α-hydroxy acid.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: The mechanism of the benzilic acid rearrangement is shown in three steps. First, a hydroxide ion attacks one of the carbonyl carbons of an α-diketone to form a tetrahedral intermediate. Second, a [1,2]-shift of the R group occurs, leading to a rearranged carboxylate. Third, a proton transfer results in the final product, an α-hydroxy acid salt.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Mechanism: The benzilic acid rearrangement is an irreversible process. The first step of the mechanism is the addition of the nucleophile (HO-, alkoxide, or amide ion) across the C=O bond to give a tetrahedral intermediate. The next step is aryl or alkyl migration to form the corresponding α-hydroxy acid salt.

scheme_candidates.nearby_text: When reacted with concentrated NaOH (50 wt%) or other strong bases (e.g., alkoxides), aliphatic and aromatic aldehydes with no α-hydrogen undergo an intermolecular hydride-transfer reaction known as the Cannizzaro reaction. In this disproportionation reaction, one molecule of aldehyde oxidizes another to the corresponding carboxylic acid and is reduced to the corresponding primary alcohol in a maximum 50% yield.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Middle of the page, under the 'Mechanism' section.
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: The mechanism depicts the addition of a hydroxide ion to an aldehyde, followed by deprotonation to form a dianionic intermediate. This intermediate then transfers a hydride to a second aldehyde molecule in the rate-determining step (RDS) to form a carboxylic acid and an alkoxide, which is subsequently protonated.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: A variety of mechanisms have been proposed for this reaction, but the generally accepted mechanism of the Cannizzaro reaction involves a hydride transfer. First, OH⁻ adds across the carbonyl group, and the resulting species is deprotonated under the applied basic conditions to give the corresponding dianion. This dianion facilitates the ability of the aldehydic hydrogen to leave as a hydride ion. This leaving hydride ion attacks another aldehyde molecule in the rate-determining step (RDS) to afford the alkoxide of a primary alcohol, which gets protonated by the solvent (H₂O).

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: An ortho-iodophenol derivative (2.3 equiv) reacts with ethyl propiolate in the presence of t-BuOCu (7 equiv), pyridine, and DME under reflux for 2h. This forms a cuprated benzofuran intermediate, which then reacts to form the final product, Isocumestan. Yields are 79% for R=H and 72% for R=t-Bu.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Synthetic Applications: In the laboratory of M. Nilsson, a facile one-pot synthesis of isocumestans (6H-benzofuro[2,3-c][1]benzopyran-6-ones) was developed via a novel extension of the Castro-Stephens coupling utilizing ortho-iodophenols and ethyl propiolate. The reaction can be regarded as an extended Castro-Stephens coupling where an intermediate cuprated benzofuran couples with a second equivalent of ortho-iodophenol, and the product lactonizes to isocumestan.

extracts.transformation_text: One-pot synthesis of Isocumestan from an ortho-iodophenol and ethyl propiolate.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Intermolecular reaction
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A substituted pyridine reacts with sodium amide or potassium amide, optionally in liquid ammonia, to yield a 2-aminopyridine derivative.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: In the early 1900s, A.E. Chichibabin reacted pyridine with sodium amide (NaNH2) in dimethylamine at high temperature (110 °C), and after aqueous work-up, he isolated 2-aminopyridine in 80% yield. A decade later, he added pyridine to powdered KOH at 320 °C, and after aqueous work-up 2-hydroxypyridine was isolated. Similar reactions take place when pyridine or its derivatives are treated with strong nucleophiles such as alkyl- and aryllithiums to give 2-alkyl and 2-arylpyridines. The direct amination of pyridine and its derivatives at their electron-deficient positions via nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) is known as the Chichibabin reaction.

extracts.transformation_text: Direct amination of a pyridine derivative at the 2-position.
extracts.conditions_text: NaNH2 or KNH2 or KNH2 / liquid NH3

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Intramolecular reaction
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A pyridine derivative with an aminoalkyl side chain undergoes intramolecular cyclization under basic, high-temperature conditions to form a fused nitrogen heterocycle.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: This reaction is also widely used for the direct introduction of an amino group into the electron-deficient positions of many azines and azoles (e.g., quinoline is aminated at C2 & C4, isoquinoline at C1, acridine at C9, phenanthridine at C6, quinazoline at C4). Both inter- and intramolecular versions are available, but investigations have mainly focused on intermolecular reactions.

extracts.transformation_text: Intramolecular amination of a pyridine derivative to form a fused bicyclic heterocycle.
extracts.reactants_text: pyridine with an N-aminoalkyl side chain
extracts.notes_text: This is an intramolecular version of the Chichibabin reaction.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Full mechanism scheme
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: The mechanism of the Chichibabin amination reaction is depicted, starting with a substituted pyridine and sodium amide forming an adsorption complex. This complex then converts to an anionic σ-complex, which subsequently loses dihydrogen to form a sodium salt. An aqueous work-up then yields the final 2-aminopyridine derivative.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Mechanism. The Chichibabin reaction is formally the nucleophilic aromatic substitution of hydride ion (H-) by the amide ion (NH2-). In the first step, an adsorption complex is formed with a weak coordination bond between the nitrogen atom in the heterocycle and the sodium ion (Na+); this coordination increases the positive charge on the ring α-carbon atom, and thus facilitates the formation of an anionic σ-complex that can be observed by NMR in liquid ammonia solution. This σ-complex is then aromatized to the corresponding sodium salt while hydrogen gas (H2) is evolved (a proton from an amino group reacts with the leaving group hydride ion).

extracts.transformation_text: Overall mechanism for the conversion of a substituted pyridine to a 2-aminopyridine derivative via nucleophilic aromatic substitution.
extracts.intermediates_text: adsorption complex, anionic σ-complex, sodium salt

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A two-step reaction sequence shows the conversion of a 1°, 2°, or 3° alcohol to an alkyl xanthate, followed by pyrolysis to yield an alkene. The first step involves reaction with a base (NaOH, KOH, or NaH) and CS2, followed by an alkyl iodide (R4-I). The second step is heating the resulting alkyl xanthate at 100-250 °C, which produces the alkene, O=C=S, and R4-SH.
scheme_candidates.caption_text: 1°, 2°, or 3° alcohol; R4 = usually CH3; alkyl xanthate; Alkene
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The formation of olefins by pyrolysis (100-250 °C) of the corresponding xanthates (containing at least one β-hydrogen atom) via cis-elimination is known as the Chugaev elimination reaction. This transformation was discovered by L. Chugaev in connection with his studies on the optical properties of xanthates1 in 1899. Xanthates are prepared from the corresponding alcohols (1°, 2°, and 3°) by first deprotonating the alcohol with a base (e.g., NaH, NaOH, or KOH) and reacting the resulting alkoxide with carbon disulfide. The metal xanthate is then trapped with an alkyl iodide (often methyl iodide).

extracts.transformation_text: Formation of an alkyl xanthate from an alcohol
extracts.reagents_text: 1. NaOH or KOH or NaH; CS2; 2. R4-I
extracts.notes_text: R4 = usually CH3

extracts.transformation_text: Pyrolysis of an alkyl xanthate to an alkene

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: The mechanism of the Chugaev elimination is shown, starting from an alkyl xanthate. The molecule undergoes heating, leading to a six-membered cyclic transition state. This transition state collapses to form an alkene and an unstable intermediate, which then decomposes into O=C=S and H-SR4.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The Chugaev reaction is an intramolecular syn elimination (Ei), and it proceeds through a six-membered transition state involving a cis-β-hydrogen atom of the alcohol moiety and the thione sulfur atom of the xanthate. Isotopic studies involving 34S and 13C showed that the C=S, and not the thiol sulfur atom, closes the ring in the transition state. The β-hydrogen and the xanthate group must be coplanar in the cyclic transition state.

extracts.transformation_text: Intramolecular syn elimination of an alkyl xanthate via a cyclic transition state

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A multi-step synthetic sequence for the preparation of (-)-Kainic acid is depicted. The key step shown is a one-pot Chugaev elimination followed by an intramolecular ene reaction. A complex cyclic xanthate is treated with NaHCO3 in refluxing PhOPh to form a tricyclic intermediate via a diene intermediate (shown in brackets). This tricyclic compound is then converted to (-)-Kainic acid in subsequent steps.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: A concise route to (-)-kainic acid was developed by K. Ogasawara and co-workers by employing sequentially a Chugaev syn-elimination and an intramolecular ene reaction as the key steps. After preparing the xanthate under standard conditions, the compound was heated to reflux in diphenyl ether in the presence of sodium bicarbonate. The desired tricyclic product bearing the trisubstituted pyrrolidine framework was formed as a single diastereomer in 72% yield.

extracts.transformation_text: Sequential Chugaev elimination and intramolecular ene reaction in the synthesis of (-)-kainic acid
extracts.notes_text: The final product, (-)-Kainic acid, is obtained after further 'steps'.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Synthesis of Dihydroclerodin
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A polycyclic alcohol is converted to a xanthate ester using NaH, CS2, and MeI. This intermediate is then heated in n-dodecane at 216 °C for 48 hours to yield an alkene via Chugaev elimination, which is further processed to form Dihydroclerodin. The overall yield for the two steps is 74%.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: In the late stages of the total synthesis of dihydroclerodin, A. Groot and co-workers used the Chugaev elimination reaction to install an exocyclic double bond on ring A. Before employing the xanthate ester pyrolysis, the authors tried several methods that failed to convert the primary alcohol to the exocyclic methylene functionality. The corresponding xanthate ester was prepared followed by heating to 216 °C in n-dodecane for 2 days to afford the desired alkene in 74% yield.

extracts.transformation_text: Conversion of a primary alcohol to a xanthate ester
extracts.notes_text: This is the first step of a two-step sequence.

extracts.transformation_text: Pyrolysis of a xanthate ester to form an exocyclic alkene
extracts.notes_text: The final product shown, Dihydroclerodin, is formed after subsequent steps.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Synthesis of (-)-Solanapyrone E
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A secondary alcohol on a decalin ring is converted to a xanthate ester using t-BuOK, CS2, and MeI in THF with 91% yield. The xanthate is then pyrolyzed in 1-methylnaphthalene at 190 °C to form a diene, which is subsequently reduced with LAH to an alcohol intermediate en route to (-)-Solanapyrone E.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: During the first total synthesis of (-)-solanapyrone E by H. Hagiwara et al., it was necessary to install the C3-C4 double bond in the decalin ring of the natural product by eliminating the C4 secondary alcohol. Since the stereochemistry of the xanthate pyrolysis is syn, it was possible to install this double bond regioselectively, without observing any of the undesired C4-C5 double bond. The C4 alcohol was first converted to the xanthate in 91% yield using t-BuOK as a base. The double bond at C3 was then selectively introduced by heating the xanthate at 190 °C in 1-methylnaphthalene.

extracts.transformation_text: Conversion of a secondary alcohol to a xanthate ester
Pyrolysis of a xanthate ester to form a diene
extracts.notes_text: This elimination is followed by a reduction step with LAH.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Synthesis of Ellacene
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A polycyclic diol (R=OH) is converted to a bis-xanthate (X=OC(=S)SMe) using CS2/NaH and MeI. The bis-xanthate is then pyrolyzed in HMPA at 220-230 °C to afford Ellacene via a double Chugaev elimination.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: J.M. Cook and co-workers accomplished the total synthesis of ellacene (1,10-cyclododecanotriquinancene) by utilizing the Weiss reaction and the Chugaev elimination as key steps. The elimination of the tris-xanthate was performed in HMPA at 220-230 °C in very high yield. This pyrolysis was superior to the elimination conducted under neat conditions.

extracts.transformation_text: Conversion of a diol to a bis-xanthate ester
Pyrolysis of a bis-xanthate ester to form Ellacene
extracts.notes_text: The nearby text mentions a 'tris-xanthate', but the scheme depicts a bis-xanthate derived from a diol.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Synthesis of a tetrahydrofluorenone precursor for Kinamycins
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A complex tricyclic xanthate ester is pyrolyzed at 300 °C under reduced pressure (20 mmHg) via Kugelrohr distillation to yield tetrahydrofluorenone in 100% yield. This is a step in the synthesis of kinamycin antibiotics.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Synthetic studies on kinamycin antibiotics in the laboratory of T. Ishikawa resulted in the elaboration of the highly oxygenated D ring with all the required stereocenters for the kinamycin skeletons. The tricyclic tertiary alcohol was converted to the corresponding xanthate and then smoothly pyrolyzed under reduced pressure to yield the desired tetrahydrofluorenone system.

extracts.transformation_text: Pyrolysis of a tertiary xanthate ester to form an alkene
extracts.notes_text: The nearby text indicates the xanthate was formed from a tertiary alcohol prior to this pyrolysis step.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: meso-octamethylcalix[4]pyrrole reacts with Cl3CCO2Na in 1,2-dimethoxyethane to produce a mixture of chlorinated calix[m]pyridine[n]pyrrole products, including calix[2]pyridine[2]pyrrole, calix[3]pyridine[1]pyrrole, and calix[4]pyridine, with yields ranging from 26-65%.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: For a long time heterocyclic analogues of calix[4]arene such as calix[4]pyridines were unknown. In the laboratory of J.L. Sessler, a universal and easy synthetic protocol was devised for the preparation of calix[m]pyridine-[n]pyrrole (m+n=4) and calix[4]pyridine systems based on the nonmetal mediated ring expansion of pyrrole. The reaction of dichlorocarbene with meso-octamethyl-calix[4]pyrrole brought about a pyrrole ring expansion to give chlorocalixpyridinopyrroles and chlorocalixpyridines. Using 15 equivalents of sodium trichloroacetate as the carbene source and 1,2-dimethoxyethane as the solvent afforded a 1:1:1 ratio of calix[3]pyridine[1]pyrrole : calix[4]pyridine : calix[2]pyridine[2]pyrrole. Only monochlorinated pyridines were formed but each pyridine ring gave rise to two regioisomers. Yields were between 26-65%.

extracts.transformation_text: Nonmetal mediated ring expansion of pyrrole units in meso-octamethylcalix[4]pyrrole to form chlorocalixpyridines.
extracts.notes_text: The reaction produces a mixture of regioisomers and different ring expansion products.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: 2,3-dimethyl-1H-indole reacts with dichlorocarbene ([:CCl2]) to yield two products: 3-chloro-2,4-dimethylquinoline (32%) and 3-(dichloromethyl)-2,3-dimethyl-3H-indole (46%).
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The first example for the insertion of an electrogenerated dichlorocarbene into substituted indoles was described by F. De Angelis and co-workers. The dichlorocarbene was generated by reduction of CCl4, followed by fragmentation of the resulting trichloromethyl anion. Under these conditions, 2,3-dimethylindole was converted to 3-chloro-2,4-dimethylquinoline and 3-(dichloromethyl)-2,3-dimethyl-3H-indole in moderate yield. The study revealed that the reaction mechanism and product formation are determined by the acidity of the solvent.

extracts.transformation_text: Insertion of dichlorocarbene into 2,3-dimethyl-1H-indole to form 3-chloro-2,4-dimethylquinoline and 3-(dichloromethyl)-2,3-dimethyl-3H-indole.
extracts.notes_text: Dichlorocarbene was electrogenerated by reduction of CCl4.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A generalized mechanism shows a substituted pyrrole reacting with dichlorocarbene ([:CCl2]) to form a cyclopropane intermediate, which then rearranges through a cationic species to yield a substituted pyridine and a dichloromethyl-substituted pyrrole.
